next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The Meridian delegation arrived on a Tuesday morning, three black SUVs grinding up the mountain road through overnight snow. Kai watched them from the cafeteria windows while he ate breakfast one-handed, fork in his right, coffee cooling untouched beside him. The vehicles pulled into the loading bay with coordinated timing, one after another, as if they'd practiced the spacing. Three people climbed out of the first: two men in dark coats and a woman in a grey wool suit who crossed the icy concrete without slipping, a leather portfolio held flat against her chest.

"That's Jin-seo Park," Tomas said across the table, leaning forward to follow Kai's gaze. "Athlete liaison. She was at the Seoul orientation last year." He paused with his spoon over a bowl of porridge. "She's all right. She actually listens."

Kai kept watching the woman in grey. Behind her, the rear doors opened on the second SUV and more people stepped out with hard cases, laptop bags, a flat archive box the size of a legal file. Nobody came up the mountain with that much paper to make life easier.

Outside, the sky was the flat grey of late February at 2,100 meters, snow stacked along the ridgelines in wind-carved drifts. The second and third SUVs emptied out behind the first: technicians, lawyers, or whatever Meridian sent when it wanted to remind people who ran the network inside their nerves. The noise in the cafeteria thinned around the windows. Someone two tables over said, very quietly, "Fuck."

Tomas watched with a different expression now, attentive and measuring, the look of someone keepi...
open_hooks_to_carry: Dinner conversation about home—Indiana flatlands, the Meridian developmental program, what homesickness means when you have never had a baseline home
Walking outside into the Alpine night—cold, stars, mountains, beauty that exists outside the crisis
prev_tail_excerpt:
...n depth, manual overrides, suppressed alerts, every extra minute Margot had stayed in the channel after coaching stopped and appetite took over. They would show the places where coaching had ended and she had not. They would show the second vector. They would also show exactly what kind of coach she was.

She pictured handing all of that over and felt the stupid animal part of herself recoil, not from guilt, from loss. There would be no feed, no borrowed lungs, no legs loading under her before a jump, no clean hit of someone else's body arriving stronger and sharper than her own.

In the corridor outside, someone's door slammed. Margot didn't move.

The phone was still lit in one hand. The cuff was in the other.

She could call Petra instead. Petra was medical. Petra cared about damage. But Petra was also thorough enough to peel the whole thing open, the kind of doctor who would ask for the logs, read every line, see Tomás at 205, see the caution bands overridden, and see that Margot had known there was another transmission route and had waited anyway.

She saw Marcus with his hand over his ear, Sonja opening and closing her fist, Kai's face held together with butterfly closures. All of them were real. So was the want.

Margot put the cuff back in the drawer.

Then she put the phone beside it and shut both away.

Her hand stayed on the wood a second too long. The tremor in her fingers did not stop. Neither did the wetness in her mouth.

She lay back on the bed fully dressed and stared into the dark, thinking about the first second after connect, and she did not call anyone.

setups_to_plant.description: Kai begins small private experiments with his healing hand, testing whether he can consciously feel and override the synthetic muscle's autonomous firing

state_deltas_required: The aftermath of crisis creating space for unexpected human connection
A genuine human connection forms between two people whose humanity is in question
